speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went into Sokolow, and he said, you're not moving from here until we are going to take care of you. He called over the nurses. Two nurses he called over-- they should bandage me. Take it off and wash it up and bandage me. They took off the bandage what I was wearing. They jumped away from me. Excuse me. I was smelled terrible from the pus and everything. They couldn't stay near me. Oh, but they washed me up. They cleaned me up, and they put a bandage on here. And I had to sit and wait until they're going to cook up that meal for the soldiers. And I said, I don't want it. The soup, the thin soup from the top, you're going to get that and drink a little bit. You're going to feel better. And I was sitting about two hours, you know, until they cooked it and made everything. And they gave me this. And they said, go. Go into the town, and maybe you're going to find somewhere there in the town. I came into town, and I found some people hidden. They are liberated already. And we sat down, and everybody started telling their story how he remained, how he remained. And I also started. There was no tears in our eyes. We are going to be the representative in the [SPEAKING FOREIGN LANGUAGE]. And from over there I went to Siemiatycze. I went to Siemiatycze. And Siemiatycze is a shtetl. I came in-- this is close to the holidays, Rosh Hashanah and I came up there, and I felt I'm sick, I can't-- I had found already a few Jews there. And I said, [SPEAKING FOREIGN LANGUAGE]. Tell me something. And there is a drugstore here. I don't know if he's going to help you, but go over. I didn't walk, I crawled. I couldn't stand on my feet. [SOBBING] I crawled over to the drug store, and I sat there crying, help me. Kill me, I said. I beg you. Kill me. Give me some medicine. I should die in this place. I cannot live anymore. They looked at me and said, look, I'm going to give you some medication. I don't know if that's going to help you or not. This is a medication we are giving to horses, we're giving this kind of medication. Either they live or they die.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he gave me the medication, and I took that medication. And it took a few hours, and I start to feel my stomach is-- I'm breathing. I can pick up myself. I remained there. Being there I heard there's some guy from Treblinka there in a hospital. His name was Weissman. Where? In which hospital? There's a hospital, a field hospital. So I picked up myself and I came up to him and said, Weissman-- he had a bullet pass by the heart. And he remained, and it didn't kill him. After that, they took him to the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we found out, you see, being there, this is characteristic. We were Rosh Hashanah, it was to Drohiczyn and Siemiatycze was one town from the other was about 20 kilometers one town to the other. And I was there one town, this was Rosh Hashanah, and I celebrated the Rosh Hashanah in that town.

speaker: interviewer
text: After Rosh Hashanah, I picked up myself, and I'm going back to the other town because I know that the other town is more you can find out. Come ahead, come to that town. What are you doing here? They say it's Rosh Hashanah. I just celebrated Rosh Hashanah here, and you're telling me that's now Rosh Hashanah? Yeah, that's Rosh Hashanah now. Not yesterday was not Rosh Hashanah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remained in that town, and the people they helped me a lot, very much. They start to-- the farmers in that place which is small and they got exchange. They gave him this, they gave him a chicken, they gave him eggs. And I was the lucky one. You see, if they got an egg, they gave me an egg. If they got some milk, they gave me a little milk. And I was with them quite a time. Then I decided I'm going to go back to Otwock. I want to see who's remain alive there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I just ask you one more question about the liquidation of the camp? Were all of the people shot in the same graves?

speaker: interviewee
text: No there was a few barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: They told you to drop your pants and to walk over towards the graves.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here was laying a group. Over there was laying a group. Over there is laying a group. Wherever there was groups of people, they were held in that place. You couldn't move from one place to the other. They picked up 20 people in a group and send them out to be shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were all the people shot in the same pits?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same. They're laying in the same grave. And I'm fighting with my friends what they gave $1 million to the Holocaust Museum. And I told him, if you can give $1 million for that Holocaust Museum, you can give $10,000 we should put up a stone laying there. [SOBBING] Hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of people laying there. That's empty. There's an empty lot there in Treblinka where they were shot. They are laying there. He told me-- he was there-- he said there's a little stone there or something-- they should not work in that place. Hundreds of people they're laying there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that spot where the graves were is not where they have the memorial, the monuments now at Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They have the memorials in the Treblinka where they buried them over there. This is a separate, completely separate place.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. thank you. OK, so you went back to Otwock. How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did I get there? I hitchhiked. The Russian, I ride with the tractor, with the car. Moronic. and I came into that Otwock, and I found a few guys which were also sent to some camps and remain alive. They were already start to do some business with them. And the whole Prague, you see, Warsaw was not taking at that time. Warsaw was under occupation. Prague was under the Russian. Warsaw was still under the German. Then when the Poles made their uprising, I've been there in Otwock. Then after the uprising from the ward from the Poles in the Warsaw, I came in to see coming from Otwock into Warsaw, and I walked up the streets and I said, mine God, [NON-ENGLISH]. [SIGHS] Something came off my chest. I felt that this is good, that a good thing. I remained there. I was in Warsaw not long. Lodz was taken. I know my mother was in Lodz. My brother lived in Lodz. I have the whole family in Lodz. I picked up-- I was in Lublin. First of all I was in Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before Otwock?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before Otwock I went to Lublin. Slept in Peretz House. You must hear about that Peretz House a lot, yes? I was there in Peretz House with a few people. Then I found [NON-ENGLISH] in Lublin. He looked at me, and then I showed him. For him I showed place. Wherever he went. Look at him. He's alive. Look at here. He was shot, and he's alive. He's alive. I was here by [NON-ENGLISH] Blumenfeld, his wife. I was in New York with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back to Otwock, did you find out what happened to your wife and your child? You knew it already.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew already where they are. After Lodz, I came back. After Lodz. We exhumed them from the-- I knew that place they were shot near that camp, in Karczew, near that camp. In that grave was like 19 people. They told me who was there, and I knew all the 19 people what was there buried.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you exhumed the bodies. Where did you rebury them?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the Jewish cemetery in Karczew. That's the cemetery in Karczew. I didn't do it. We hired two people. We went in to a funeral parlor in Otwock, and we told them, look, we know where the people are laying. We don't want them to be on a field laying there. I don't know what's going to happen. Make two coffins. Whatever the remnants from them, we're going to put them in the coffins, and we're going to bury them in the Jewish cemetery. My wife's brother he's the Polish army. So we went there with the people from the funeral parlor, and we showed them where they were buried. About two or three people, and they start to dig out. I recognized that little shoe from that little boy [NON-ENGLISH]. And in that grave was her father, her sister, she, and the baby was in that grave. And the other people I know the names because the people from that camp they told me who's there. We put them in the two coffins. And took then to the cemetery, and we buried them in the cemetery. Then went to Lublin. I was in Lublin quite a time. And Tuka was also there in Lublin. And we found a place where to live. We get together with some boys there and there, and we are one together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your goal? What were you aiming to do?

words.text: What were you aiming to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My goal, I was no goal at that time. I don't know what I'm doing, where I'm going, what I'm going to further. I didn't have nothing. I only want to find out if I have somebody from the family. That's all. I know that my sister-in-law, she live in Warsaw. I was with her husband quite a while in the ghetto. He remained. I was with her husband. Then during the uprising I don't know where he disappeared. Everybody disappeared. From Lublin, I picked up, we hitchhiked to Lodz in a tractor. Took us two days, and we came in to Lodz. Came into Lodz and I know the address where my brother lived. In the morning I went there. Nothing. I was looking for a picture, something to find. You're going to ask me why I have this picture. My sister, my older sister was in Russia during the occupation. She was in Russia. She remained, and she had these pictures. After the war she gave me the picture of my mother. See that's a little picture of my mother. My daughter made a little picture. She did it at the Saks Fifth Avenue. She made an enlargement from the picture. And I looked around Lodz, there and there and there. I couldn't find, but I met my wife here. She was liberated in January 1945 from Czestochowa. And I was walking in the street there with a friend of mine which he was also in Treblinka. And I walk with him, and this girl passed by. She was wearing a coat on the back of still a mark from that camp, from Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my friend, he knew her. He knew my wife. He said, oh, Helinka, are you alive? You must have-- a lot of Polishmen-- are you alive? He said, come with me to where I live. Next door is an empty apartment. A German was living there. Private, piano, everything left. He said, you can move in.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went. We looked up a nice-- and she didn't have where to go. They were very rich. Her parents in Lodz were very rich people. Manufacturers from all kind of stuff, materials. They had a beautiful home there with six rooms with everything. And I said to her when he gave me the apartment, if you want you can. There are so many rooms here. She said, no, I'm not moving in with nobody. She went away, and I made up with her we should meet the next day we're going to talk. Meanwhile, her brother was also in Czestochowa, and she found out in Lodz that he is free, and he is there. So she went to him, and she found him. And after quite a few days I said, now you have your brother, you can move from mine apartment. You have your brother. You have security. She moved in, and we started so-so. And this was in the beginning of '45. Not right in the beginning. She was liberated. The whole time we were in Lodz. We try it. This, this, you know, to do something. And she was living then. Her brother is there. Then I met a couple which they were living near Treblinka. After Treblinka, we stopped in that little town, and I saw her in the town. Walking in Lodz, I saw and said, I know you from somewhere. Yeah, you were-- yeah. So she moved in. I had such a big apartment, I took her in. She was carrying her bundle on her back with her mother, and I took her into that apartment. We were there in the apartment until about the end of '45. By the end of '45 I said, this is not the place where to be. We have to go look for a future. We are free. We have to do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: My wife, she said, what are you going to do? I said, let's try to get into Berlin. Berlin is a Jewish committee which they are taking care of immigration with everything. And I have an uncle in Argentina. My father's brother lives in Argentina. I will write to them maybe, but I don't know that. But we will write and we will find out. Then we will see what we have to do. When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in beginning of when I met her. The Jewish wedding we had in Stuttgart. It's a separate. She didn't want to live with me not knowing. She didn't want to be with me period. That's all right. I said, let's go over to the magistrate and register ourself as the man and wife. When we had to cut some document and we are man and wife, and we both were together. From Lodz, I said this is not the place where I'm going to live. I don't want to live here. I don't want to be there. I want to go there with all the refugees. The refugees are in Berlin. It was a big place there. We couldn't. I was afraid to take her with me we should travel unlegal on the train. It was some Russian soldiers which they got for a few dollars they took them in the cars. So I found out from somebody they're here. You give him so much and so much and he's going to take it. I said, Helen, whatever that's going to happen with me, you are here. Come. I will try to take care of you. You should be there in Berlin. If I'm going to be alive and I will come to Berlin, then we will decide, both of us, what to do. Meanwhile we are registered. Helen and Simon. And we are registered. We have a little legal comp document.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I paid that guy, and he put her in a Russian [NON-ENGLISH]. And I put her on that car with another few people, not by herself. And he went to Berlin. To I don't know where in Berlin, but we know we are going to find each other by the Jewish committee somewhere. This is the only place where we can find each other. That's what you made up. She left.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days later, I decided I'm not going to walk around by myself. So I went over to the railroad train and bought a ticket. I bought a ticket. I don't know. I went into a car where a lot of Russian soldiers were sitting there. I sat down, and I laid down. So I covered myself, and I know that the train is going to Berlin. This I knew it, because my aim was to go to Berlin and to find her. On the road they checked that I was sitting, and I came into Berlin. I came into Berlin. And I went to the Jewish committee there, and I found her. I found her in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: We went in the Zehlendorf, a place where the refugees was hiding themselves. And I was there a few days. Then we decided what we're going to do in Berlin? Berlin is just a place where to pass by. We're going to go to Stuttgart. Stuttgart is already Jewish committees and everything. We're going to go to Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewee
text: We picked up ourselves with another couple which they live also here in Florida. And we are going to go to Stuttgart. We came to Stuttgart. Came in the [NON-ENGLISH] that's full of Jews, Jewish committee and everything. They gave us a place where to live. We moved into the room, and Helen decided that if you want to live with me, we have to have a Jewish chassanah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We got together a few people, and we made our legal Jewish. Then I said, we have to do something-- wouldn't stay here in Stuttgart, and Stuttgart is not a place where to live. We're going to finish our life here in Stuttgart?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard, oh, they're going to Israel, they're going to America, they are going here. But we had a representative from the Joint, a Jewish woman. Mrs. Levine. I know the name was Mrs. Levine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Levine, the case is like this. My wife is here. I'm here. I think she's pregnant, and I don't want her to have her child here, for no money in the world in Germany. A German? She shouldn't be born in Germany. She shouldn't be born in Germany. She was very arrogant, the lady, so what do you want me to do? I want you to send me. I know you are sending people to the United States, and I want you to send me to the United States. She want to-- So she said, I cannot send you. You have somebody in the United States? I have you. That's enough for me. Who do I need? You are the representative, my representative. No. I said to her, I'm going to go to the American consul and I'm going to-- so you want to go, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I picked up myself and I take my wife and we went to the American consul in Stuttgart. And I came in and I told them the whole story of how I got liberated, how I got free, how I met my wife. [SOBBING] I don't want to have a child here. Do whatever you want. Send me wherever you want. I don't want our child here. Mr. Friedman, I give you papers. Go to Mrs. Levine. Tell Mrs. Levine she should fill out the papers, and with the next transport you're going, the next ship you're leaving to America. [LAUGHING] I came to Mrs. Levine to fill out the papers. Oh no, I'm not doing this. This is not my job. You go back. I'm picking it up myself and I'm going right away to the consul. Tell the consul that you don't want to obey his orders. And I start to walk away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Friedman, don't rush so. Don't rush. And she filled out the papers. During 14 days I was with my wife on the ship. I came to America. I haven't got nobody. Where shall I go? I get-- both of us-- we get off the ship. She's pregnant. And I'm looking at her, she looks at me. I don't see people. I don't see nobody. Where did they disappeared, the whole ship  of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I'm walking, some Jew passed by, and I stop him. I said in Yiddish, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. He said, don't worry. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. Get back up in the [NON-ENGLISH]. He says, don't worry. [NON-ENGLISH]. Two minutes later, I see girls walking around. A girl comes over to me. You just came from the ship. Yeah. They looked at her and she's-- they took her almost on the hand they carried her almost led. They set her down. You sit. Don't do anything. Don't you dare do anything. Don't pick up anything. Everything we'll take care of. We're going to take care of you. You are going to be all right. They took us from the ship. We went into the Hotel Marseilles in New York, 103rd Street and Broadway. The whole ship. People which they didn't have where to go, they gave us a room, an apartment. Beautiful. 10th floor, 15th floor. We are in the room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And downstairs we came into the hotel was served decked up tables with food and all kind of things, and people standing on the side. They're coming from concentration camp. Got horns with how they look. We look decent people, you know? Because we were already quite a time after the liberation, I took my jacket I have a bundle at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came up to the room. We came up to that room. We didn't have the telephone. Then we found out. In the room be heard ringing, ringing, we didn't know. Finally, we found out that a telephone is under the bed. The telephone under the bed. We pick up the telephone.

speaker: interviewer
text: We came with another friend, another girl. She lives now in California. She called me, Shimon, Helen. What are you doing? I'm here with my family. She came to rich family, rich ones. Park Avenue in New York. They have apartments and a jewelry business. And I says, what do you mean, What you are doing here? You have a rich family, and I came here. I don't have nobody. Are you asking me what I am doing here? She said, I have an apartment already. Already apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And meanwhile I had an apartment on Stelton Street for $14 a month. It was a condemned building, completely a condemned building, but you couldn't move into that building. We are a few people that we went into that building. That's all right.
